speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 12, 1996. I am the interviewer, Mark Goldberg, interviewing Chief Prosecutor William Dowdell Denson in Lawrence, NY, USA. Language, English. This is tape number five. We're about to do a series of pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is made-- or was made-- at Dachau, at what we called the arraignment of the accused in the Dachau case. The accused was sitting in front of Paul Goethe, and Paul Goethe is reading the charges, in German, upon which these 40 accused were being tried in the Dachau case. This was a part of a necessary criminal procedure. It was being given to these accused in their own language so that they knew the details of the offenses of which they were being brought to trial. And that's in accordance with the practice in the courts of this country, an arraignment of the accused.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph discloses the prisoners, or the accused, in the Mauthausen trial. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were standing out at a recess that was declared twice during each trial day. One in the morning, at about 10:30, and the second in the afternoon, at about 3:00. And the recess lasted for about 30 minutes, and gave a chance to all participants to stretch their legs and relax as far as they could. This view is of the prisoners standing in front of the barracks in which they were confined. The iron bars-- the steel bars that you see there were the bars that were in the courtroom. The prisoners were under the supervision of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] infantryman from Poland-- Polish guards-- and they were the London Poles that filled the capacity of guarding the prisoners here at Dachau. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This photograph was-- discloses a scene in the courtroom during the actual proceedings. It was taken by photographers of the Signal Corps of the United States Army, or other designated persons of-- who were engaged in similar activities. It shows a picture of my staff there. We have on my immediate right is Paul Goethe. Next to him was Colonel [? Markham, ?] and the gentleman next to Colonel [? Markham ?] is Captain [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? Lane. ?] And there is a fourth officer there whose name, at this juncture, slips me. I guess I can check it out and tell you at a little later time. But this is an actual scene of-- that was in existence during the trial of the Mauthausen case. This is not posed. It was taken in the regular course of business of recording what transpired, as are all of these photos, of what transpired at various times during the prosecution of these cases at Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all the black and white-- are all the black and white photographs we're seeing taken by official Army photographers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were indeed. There were some that were taken-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, I don't know of any right at this moment that were not taken by official photographers that were designated for the purpose of taking these pictures to make a record of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph discloses Major General Fay Prickett on the left, and Colonel Rosenfeld on the right. General Prickett was the President of the Court, and presided at the trial of the Mauthausen concentration camp case. And Colonel Rosenfeld was the law member. Each of these courts had a law member. That is, a person who was learned in the law who advised the court on the matters of law applicable to the matter before it. And it was a function of the balance of the court to ascertain what were the facts, and apply to those facts the law as given to them by the law member. In that way, you had the findings of fact and conclusions of law and the sentences that were handed down by the court. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photograph that was taken during the course of the Mauthausen trial and accurately represents the proceedings at that particular instant in time. It discloses the presence of the chief prosecutor, standing up on the witness's left. In other words, a witness was on the stand and was being interrogated by me, who was standing at the witness's left. On the witness's right is the interpreter. We did not have instantaneous translations. We had a series of interpreters, and where the witness was German, the question would be asked by the prosecutor in English. It was then translated into German for the benefit of the accused and the witness. And then the witness would make answer, and then the translation back of that answer would be made from the interpreter of the German into English. And this is an actual photograph of what actually happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This photograph was taken during the actual proceedings in the Mauthausen trial. You can see in the background the accused, who was over in the prisoner's dock. Each of them had a number that he-- that was assigned to him throughout the entire trial. In the foreground here, we have the backs of Colonel Rosenfeld sitting next to General Prickett, and then there are two other colonels in the view. And they are talking to Lieutenant McMahon and to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were discussing a matter with the court that related to a subject matter that I don't recall at this particular time, but from time to time it was necessary to iron out, or smooth procedures. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Certain aspects that related to the presentation of evidence, and as such, we would confer with the court, defense counsel, and the prosecution. And this is the same as occurs in our trials today. Well, this is equivalent of a bench conference would be the language that the lawyers in this country would understand as what was transpiring here-- a bench conference. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photograph discloses a picture of the court in the Mauthausen concentration camp case. In the foreground, you see a stack of books. Those were known as the death books. The Germans were very methodical in the keeping of records that they wanted to maintain, and the death books were one of the-- of those records that happened not to be destroyed when the camp was liberated. And they contain the names of the prisoners whose death had occurred, the date and, if they were shot, they were still entered as heart failure as the cause of death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This photograph also discloses [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another view of the personnel of the court in the Mauthausen case. You can see there four colonels that line the picture in the foreground. General Prickett's in the middle of the left-- on the left hand side. And there's only one-- you can't see the other three colonels that comprise the court. These men, as I say, were all men who had served considerable time and had experience in the activities of court martial and of fact finding. The picture discloses a little bit of the dais in which they sat to hear the case. In the background, you can barely see it, but there are one of two figures there which were translators and interpreters for the court. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This photograph presents a view of the courtroom in which the Mauthausen concentration camp case was tried. In the foreground are seats for the spectators. And this courtroom was made from a converted building that the SS used during the war to make shoes and trousers for the troops. As I said, the spec-- it was about 200 feet from the American flag that you see there behind the court to the door which is behind you as you look at this photograph. The white helmeted individuals are the Polish guards that maintained the order. And the bench [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dais was there in plain view, and was occupied by the members of the court. That's a Third Army symbol over on the right hand side and on the left hand side of the wall of that court.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say spectators. Was there also press there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was-- there were press and we-- we had a lot of coverage before Nuremberg started in the Dachau case. And when Nuremberg ended, then they came back to us and reported our trials. But we were no competition for the press when Nuremberg was holding its proceedings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was represented in the press? What-- what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had AP and UP. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was the German-- I can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Reuters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reuters. Reuters. And they had the English-- I don't recall the names now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were a number of other countries represented?

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were practically-- practically every country in Europe was represented by the press there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photograph was taken during the course of the Mauthausen trial and discloses [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the prosecutor-- me-- interrogating a witness by the name of Orenstein. I recall now that Orenstein was one of the prisoners who operated down in the crematory. And I hadn't seen Orenstein [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] since the trial until he showed up at my office in Mineola, of Meltzer, Lippe, Goldstein, [? Wolfe ?], Schlissel, and [? Saisor. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until October of last year, I haven't seen him for years. And unfortunately [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the man died. He had a heart attack and had survived Mauthausen, and went to Philadelphia and opened a very nice business there. Came up and visited us there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the firm at Mineola. It was-- he was a dear friend of mine and helped me a lot in the preparation of the Mauthausen trial. This picture discloses Orenstein, O-R-E-N-S-T-E-I-N, as a witness, and the interpreter and a guard and some spectators. But it was an actual photograph of what transpired there during the Mauthausen trial. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This photograph discloses [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a picture of Ilse Koch, who was one of the accused in the Buchenwald trial. She was the wife of a commandant at Buchenwald, and was one-- she was the only female that we prosecuted. She was a very interesting person. At the time that this picture was taken, during the course of the Buchenwald trial, she was four months pregnant and had been in custody for-- of the Americans as a potential defendant in these cases for quite a while. On the right hand side of the picture, you see some of the accused in the Buchenwald case, and the Polish guards who were standing by to maintain order. And Ilse chose to take the stand and testify on her own behalf. And she made a good witness-- as good, certainly, as could be done under the circumstances, which were very trying because she was, indeed, a bitch. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This photograph discloses [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] August Eigruber, who was a Gauleiter of Upper Austria and one of the accused in the Mauthausen trial. I was cross-examining him, and he was really the most contemptuous and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unlikeable character that you could find anywhere. And the whole purpose of my cross-examination was to try to accentuate his contempt for the court, and for the United States, and everybody else. He was smart. He was young. As a Gauleiter of Upper Austria, he was one of the top Nazi rulers, and had, really, a concentration camp of his own. But we tried him for aiding, abetting, and participating in the operation of the Mauthausen concentration camp. And at the time that he was sentenced-- was carried out a sentence of death by hanging. As the hangman put the noose around his neck, he screamed 'Heil Hitler!' No contrition of any kind or character. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This view discloses the accused in the Mauthausen trial. There were 61 of them, and as you can see the numbers on placards that described the individual. In front of these accused were some of the defense counsel. And as I have tried to point out, these defense counsel was assigned by the government-- the United States government-- to the defense of these people. And they had their own lawyers if they so desired. And they were paid for by the United States government-- the-- the private lawyers. The accused occupied what was known as the dock. And in this view, they're sitting on the right hand side of the court, which would be to the right end of this picture. This is an actual photograph of-- of the proceedings as they were held there at Dachau during the period of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] March, 1946 to May, 1946. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture discloses a view of the outside of the building in which the court room was held. And it is, as you can see from the sign, the War Crimes Branch, Judge Advocate Section, the Headquarters Third United States Army. The individuals you see there are members of the court-- the Mauthausen court-- leaving the-- the building. This building was renovated from a structure that housed the machinery for making shoes and trousers for the German Armed Forces. It was re-done in many ways, and I'm informed at the present time that none of this is in existence in Germany, that it has all been razed and nothing but flowers are planted there where the court used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your own personal living quarters like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I lived high off the hog. I lived in a place called Nikolas Villa. I had nine servants, and I had a-- a formal garden, a vegetable garden, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] swimming pool, and I think about nine bedrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about security? Did you require any security, and if so, did you have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] We had security. I didn't require it. There were instances where there was some ugliness conduct, but nothing that was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing that was terribly important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no-- no problems with the surrounding civilian population?

speaker: interviewee
text: No problems with the surroundings civilians. Nikolas Villa was located on top of the highest portion of the village of Dachau. Now, I could look around for miles and miles. I could almost see Munich from the porch of the house in which I occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This view discloses the front of one of the ovens in the crematory at Flossenburg. In other words, it got so that the disposition of dead bodies was a big problem for the Germans, and they used cremation as a means for disposing of the bodies. And there were these ovens, all of which were comparable to the one that you see here, except that the numbers were different. They were-- for instance, at Mauthausen, they had about six of those ovens. At Flossenburg, this is a view of one on 'em. I know there were at least four there at Flossenburg. And at Dachau, there were about four ovens. The concentration camps were classified as Class I, II, III, and IV, Class IV being extermination centers, which meant that the prisoners that were sent to those camps were sent with an index card identifying the individual, and it contained a notation-- 'Return Not Desirable.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And believe me, the people in the camps saw to it that there were very few returns. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a view of opening day at the Mauthausen trial. On the left, you see General Truskett. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a Lieutenant General at that time, I believe. And on General Truskett's left is Colonel Charles E. Cheever. Colonel Cheever was a Staff Judge Advocate for Headquarters Third US Army. And on Colonel Cheever's left are two offices who were the aides to General Truskett. When you are a Lieutenant General, you have a lot of aides. And here the two gentlemen you see there on Colonel Cheever's left were the aides to General Lucian Truskett. General Truskett was the appointing authority for the Dachau trial and the Mauthausen trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This tape is at an end. Thank you very much.
